coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots assisted formation of DNA hydrogel for sustained release of drug
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Seema
Mishra, Anshul
Kumari, Rina
Sinha, Kislay K.
Singh, Manoj K.
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: The construction of DNA-carbon dot (CD) hybrid hydrogel for targeted and sustained release of drug molecules is reported here. Amine functionalized carbon dots were conjugated to 5′-phosphate termini of Cytosine (C) rich ssDNA by phosphoramidate linkage. As a prototype, chemotherapeutic drug Doxorubicin (Dox) was loaded and enclosed in hydrogel that acts as a container for sustained release of the drug. Apart from acting as a cross linker for network formation, CDs also participate in encapsulating the drug by electrostatic interaction along with DNA. Moreover, photophysical properties of CD potentially enable tracking of hydrogel dissolution and drug cargo loading in hydrogel. The visually detectable sol-gel transition of CD-DNA hybrid hydrogel was achieved by varying the pH of the solution from alkaline to neutral. The in vitro time and pH dependent release profile of the drug from hydrogel was studied. While hydrogel was found to be stable for a month at normal physiological pH, complete dissolution and sustained release of the drug molecules were achieved over 10–11 days in acidic pH, relevant to tumor microenvironment. The cell viability assay performed on HeLa cells shows their effective slow killing in presence of Dox loaded hydrogel owing to favorable acidic pH for hydrogel disruption.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of CDs with C-rich ssDNA and CD-DNA hydrogel formation
Covalent conjugation of CDs with ssDNA and photophysical characterization
Creation and characterization of hydrogel obtained from CD-DNA conjugate
In-vitro release profile of drug from hydrogel
Cytotoxicity assay of hydrogel with HeLa cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for conjugation of C-rich ssDNA to CDs and hydrogel formation at neutral pH for Dox encapsulation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
A. UV–visible absorbance B. Fluorescence spectra and C.TEM images of N, S co-doped CDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

A. UV–visible absorbance B. Fluorescence spectra C. 1.5% agarose gel of CDs and CD-DNA conjugate shows retarded gel mobility of CD-DNA conjugate as compared to free CDs and ssDNA and D. Circular dichroism spectra of CDs and CD-DNA conjugate at different pH. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical images of CD-DNA in solution (left) and in gel state (right) A. in white light B. in UV light and C. Frequency sweep test of CD-DNA hydrogel between 0.05 and 100 rad/s and strain of 1% at 25 °C. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Dox encapsulated CD-DNA hydrogel in white light (orange) and in UV light (pink). Leftmost tube represents CD-DNA hydrogel before Dox loading. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

A. UV–visible absorbance spectra B. Steady-state fluorescence spectra at λex = 330 nm C. Fluorescence spectra at λex = 480 nm of CD-DNA conjugate solution on addition of Dox D. Time-resolved fluorescence decay of CD-DNA conjugate and E. Time-resolved fluorescence decay of Dox containing CD-DNA conjugate. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
A. Digital images and B. Dox drug release profile from CD-DNA hydrogel with time in PBS buffer at pH 7.4 and 6. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Viability of HeLa cells treated with CD-DNA hydrogel, free Dox and Dox encapsulated CD-DNA hydrogel. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon dots assisted formation of DNA hydrogel for sustained release of drug

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Hajipur 844101, Bihar, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Atomic and Molecular Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of DNA-carbon dot (CD) hybrid hydrogel for targeted and sustained release of drug molecules is reported here. Amine functionalized carbon dots were conjugated to 5′-phosphate termini of Cytosine (C) rich ssDNA by phosphoramidate linkage. As a prototype, chemotherapeutic drug Doxorubicin (Dox) was loaded and enclosed in hydrogel that acts as a container for sustained release of the drug. Apart from acting as a cross linker for network formation, CDs also participate in encapsulating the drug by electrostatic interaction along with DNA. Moreover, photophysical properties of CD potentially enable tracking of hydrogel dissolution and drug cargo loading in hydrogel. The visually detectable sol-gel transition of CD-DNA hybrid hydrogel was achieved by varying the pH of the solution from alkaline to neutral. The in vitro time and pH dependent release profile of the drug from hydrogel was studied. While hydrogel was found to be stable for a month at normal physiological pH, complete dissolution and sustained release of the drug molecules were achieved over 10–11 days in acidic pH, relevant to tumor microenvironment. The cell viability assay performed on HeLa cells shows their effective slow killing in presence of Dox loaded hydrogel owing to favorable acidic pH for hydrogel disruption.

ce:section.ce:para: In conventional drug delivery, therapeutic efficacy may get reduced due to initial burst release of the drug followed by its suboptimal level in blood or tissues [1–3]. Conversely, sustained and targeted delivery of drug from biomedical devices is highly desirable in majority of cases [4,5]. This refers to specific interactions of the device predominantly with the target zone and long term moderate supply of drugs to ailing tissues resulting in increased therapeutic efficacy [6]. A plethora of engineered nanoscale drug delivery systems has been reported for such applications [7]. However, the challenges of solubility, selectivity, toxicity, biocompatibility and biodegradability have slithered in many occasions that may limit their uses [8]. A great deal of research efforts are being dedicated to overcome these limitations and to improve the therapeutic efficacy of drugs in various engineered nanoparticles viz. liposomes, dendrimers, hydrogels and others [9–11]. In particular, hydrogels have found many advantageous applications in material science, pharmaceutical science, chemistry and biomedical field due to their temperature and pH dependent tunable sol-gel properties [12–15]. It has been used in biosensing, encapsulation and release of drugs, enzymes and even whole cells [16,17]. The high water content, softness and porosity of hydrogels closely resemble living tissues and open up many potential applications in medical science [18,19]. Furthermore, lot of interest has been generated in direct monitoring of the drug cargo loading in hydrogel based drug delivery vehicles through simple visual screening and imaging studies, which is an extremely attractive proposition [20,21]. Herein, we report the creation of a novel pH responsive carbon dot CD-DNA hybrid hydrogel by careful selection of the DNA sequence and conjugating the same with CDs that act as core for branching and simultaneously offer the possibility of imaging based monitoring of drug loading in the hydrogel due to its inherent fluorescence.

CD is one of the latest addition to the carbon nanoparticle family with size <10 nm. CDs are water soluble, biocompatible, and inexpensive with excellent fluorescent properties and photostability [22,23]. Ever since their discovery in 2004, they are offering a promising alternative to other toxic nanoparticles [24,25]. Reportedly, CD has shown remarkable blood compatibility at low concentrations [26]. They have been considerably researched for drug delivery, gene delivery and photosensitizers [27–29]. The functional groups present on the surface of CDs impart water solubility and present excellent opportunity for conjugation with organic, inorganic, polymers and biomolecules including DNA for various theranostic applications [30–33]. An added advantage of CD is that the surface functionality could be wisely chosen to embrace selective drug molecules through electrostatic interaction. This implies that the same drug molecules that are intercalated or stacked in the grooves of DNA could also be loosely attached to CDs and could be disembarked on demand or under favorable conditions.

DNA has been established as an excellent stimuli responsive building block for hydrogels [34]. The cytosine (C)-rich DNA sequences widely found in telomeres and promoters of oncogenes can form tetraplex i-motif structures maintained by Hoogstein base CCH+ pair interactions [35,36]. In 2009, Liu and co-workers first reported the formation of DNA hydrogel from these DNA strands by supramolecular interaction of i-motif in slightly acidic conditions [37,38]. The pH responsiveness of DNA based hydrogels can be a boon. Since majority of cancer thrives in acidic environment, it provides an opportunity for the hydrogels to undergo dissolution at these selective sites and release a relevant anticancer drug cargo. However, the rigid structure of DNA-only hydrogels limits their applications due to less porosity for drug encapsulation [39]. More recently, modifications have been done to increase the mesh size of DNA hydrogels by incorporating gold nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes (CNT) and graphene as cross linkers for network formation and effective drug loading [40–42]. Despite that, high cost of gold and solubility issues of graphene and CNT may limit the wide scale biological applicability of these nanoparticles for hydrogel formation [43,44]. However, CD has emerged as a novel alternative to these nanoparticles with low cost synthesis, biocompatibility and aqueous solubility [45].

In a novel intervention, we present a green and simple methodology to create CD based hydrogel in conjugation with DNA in aqueous phase at room temperature (Fig. 1 ). As a proof of principle, the anticancer chemotherapeutic drug Doxorubicin (Dox) was chosen as drug cargo to be encapsulated in CD-DNA hybrid hydrogel. While the use of DNA ensures biocompatibility and biodegradability of the hydrogel, care has also been taken to limit the concentration of CDs below toxic level. In addition to sustained release of drug owing to the dissolution of hydrogel in acidic pH, the hydrogel presents interesting photophysical properties due to the presence of intensely fluorescent CDs and Dox that are retained in the DNA-hydrogel. Thus, the development of DNA and CD based hybrid hydrogel reported here as stimuli responsive drug carrier with an option to monitor drug cargo loading and release, presents an unprecedented opportunity to discover smart drug delivery vehicles for various applications.

Citric acid monohydrate, cysteamine, Dox and methylthiazolyldiphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. C-rich oligonucleotide DNA (11mer) was obtained from Sigma custom oligo synthesis. 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl) and 1-methyl imidazole were obtained from Alfa Aesar. HeLa cell lines, fetal calf serum (FCS) and RPMI Medium were purchased from Gibco. All chemicals were used as received. Nanopure water from Millipore was used in all experiments. The DNA sequence used is as follows:
5′- PO4-CCCCTAACCCC-3′

UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded on an UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Steady-state fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan). Time resolved fluorescence decays were measured by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer from Edinburgh Instruments (LifeSpec-II, UK) working on time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique. The size and shape of CDs were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), captured on a Hitachi (H-7500) electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Samples were prepared by dispersing 10 μL solution of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) on a carbon grid and air-dried for 20 min for imaging by TEM unit. The conformation of i-motif structure of DNA was determined by circular dichroism spectra on a J-1500 spectropolarimeter (Jasco, UK). Data were expressed as degrees of ellipticity (è), in units of millidegrees (mdeg). Each sample was scanned thrice with a scan rate of 50 nm/min with 1 mm path length. All measurements were performed in aqueous medium. Frequency sweeps rheological tests of hydrogel were carried out on a modular compact rheometer (Anton Paar MCR 302, Austria). The covalent conjugation of CDs with ssDNA was visualized by 1.5% agarose gel electrophoresis in 1X tris acetate EDTA (TAE) buffer and images were taken by a Canon D600 digital camera.

The CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid as a carbon source and cysteamine as a nitrogen and sulfur source. In a typical synthesis, citric acid (10 mmol, 2.10 g) and cysteamine (8.7 mmol, 0.64 g) were dissolved in 10 mL water and evaporated at 90 °C for 6 h. The viscous solution so obtained on heating was transferred into a teflon-lined autoclave followed by further heating at 180 °C for 6 h. After heating, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally. The resulting solution was diluted to 50 mL, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove the large agglomerated particles and washed to remove the unreacted organic moieties. The light yellow aqueous solution containing CDs was collected and filtered through 0.2 μm filter for further purification. Aqueous solution of purified CDs was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of CDs with C-rich ssDNA and CD-DNA hydrogel formation
ce:section.ce:para: 5′ phosphorylated C-rich ssDNA (0.1 μmol) was activated by addition of EDC (10 μL, 10 mM) and aqueous solution of 1-methyl imidazole (10 μL, 20 mM) at pH 6 and thoroughly mixed for 90 min at 37 °C with constant stirring. To this activated 5′- phosphorimidazolide ssDNA, 10 μL of amine terminated CDs (5 mg/mL) was added in the presence of (4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid) HEPES buffer (10 μL, 0.2 M, pH 8) and kept at 55 °C for 18 h. The product was purified by dialysis (MWCO-1 KDa) to remove the excess of EDC, 1-methyl imidazole.

For hydrogel formation, the purified CD-DNA conjugate was dissolved in (100 mM, 500 μL) NaCl buffer (pH ∼7) to give concentration of ssDNA (1 mM) and CDs (50 μg), heated to 90 °C for 5 min, cooled to room temperature, and subsequently cooled down to 4 °C slowly. Similarly, for control experiment, ssDNA without CDs was dissolved in (100 mM, 500 μL) NaCl buffer (pH ∼7) (concentration of ssDNA 1 mM) heated to 90 °C for 5 min and cooled from room temperature to 4 °C. The ssDNA without CDs remained in solution state at pH ∼7 which confirms the role of CDs as a cross linker for gel formation. Further, hydrogel concentration is given in terms of ssDNA.

The CD-DNA (1 mM ssDNA) aqueous solution was incubated with an aqueous solution of Dox (100 μL, 1 mg/mL) for 24 h and processed for gel formation. The free Dox was removed from gel by washing the gel with water several times. The drug loading capacity of the hydrogel was calculated from the absorbance of Dox in supernatant. The Dox encapsulation efficiency of CD-DNA hydrogel was calculated using equation (1). The drug-encapsulated hydrogel (5 μg Dox/mg of hydrogel containing 0.05 mM ssDNA) was used for further characterization.
(1)
Dox
encapsulation
efficiency
( % )
=
Total
Dox − Free
Dox
in
supernatant
Total
Dox
× 100

In order to study the in-vitro release of drug from hydrogel in physiological and slightly acidic conditions, the gel was immersed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) of pH 7.4 and pH 6 respectively. The supernatants were collected and replaced with fresh buffer at fixed intervals of time. The % release of drug was calculated by normalizing the total amount of drug absorbed in the gel to the drug released during different intervals of time. The digital images of gel were also taken to display the changes in size of the gel during drug release process.

The human cervical cancer cell line, HeLa was cultured in RPMI medium supplemented with 10% FCS and incubated in CO2 incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2. After attaining approximately 80% confluence, the cell monolayer was washed with PBS, and detached with trypsin/EDTA (1 mL for 25 cm2 of surface area) and incubated for 2 min in CO2 incubator. Cells were collected by adding 1 mL of fresh medium and centrifuging at 4500 rpm for 2 min. The supernatant was discarded and cells were resuspended in sufficient medium to split into four flasks.

HeLa cells (1 × 104 cells/well) were cultured in a 96-well plate at 37 °C, and incubated overnight for adherence on the surface of plate and then supernatant was discarded. The cells were treated with 20 μg/mL of Dox solution, hydrogel alone (0.2 mM ssDNA without Dox) and hydrogel containing Dox (0.2 mM ssDNA containing 20 μg Dox/mL) for 24, 48 and 72 h. Untreated cells were used as a control. The supernatant of each well was removed and washed twice with 1X PBS. After washing, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL in PBS) and 100 μL of medium (pH 6) were introduced and incubated for 4 h. The resultant formazan crystals were dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (100 μL) and the absorbance was measured by a microplate reader at 570 nm. All experiments were performed in triplicate and the relative cell viability (%) was expressed as a percentage relative to the untreated control cells.

Several methods have been applied for the synthesis of CDs such as microwave irradiation, laser ablation, and electrochemical oxidation. However, hydrothermal synthesis of CDs has been proved to be an advanced, green, facile and economic method for the synthesis of CDs with high quantum yield. Aqueous synthesis of CDs with citric acid and cysteamine as precursors was achieved by hydrothermal heating following the procedure reported by Dong et al. with some modifications [46]. The CDs were synthesized by mild heating at 180 °C for 6 h, the optimized condition for CDs with high quantum yield. At decreased temperature, less carbonization of precursors results in CDs of larger size and increased temperature lead to carbonization of surface functional groups both of which compromise the quantum yield. Similarly, increase and decrease in the optimum time as mentioned above leads to less fluorescent CDS. The CDs prepared by this method with three functional groups COOH, SH and NH2 have high solubility and stability. The quantum yield of CDs was measured using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.55) in 0.5 M H2SO4 as standard and came out to be considerably good (46%). The optical characterization of CDs was done by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectra of CDs show broad absorption from 200 to 400 nm with one prominent peak at 330 nm that is assigned to n-π∗ transition due to the presence of CO group (Fig. 2 A). The fluorescence spectra of CDs show maximum intensity at 430 nm at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The emission of CDs was found to be excitation wavelength dependent and showed red shift from 430 to 460 nm when excitation wavelength was varied from 330 to 390 nm (Fig. 2B). The effect of change in -pH from 6 to 10 was also checked which shows negligible change in fluorescent properties of CDs. The trapping of excited state energy causes strong emission from CDs in the blue region. The strong fluorescence of CDs was attributed to recombination of electron and hole on the surface of CDs. The nitrogen doping in CDs results in high yield of electron-hole recombination process due to a new kind of surface states known as N-state [47,48]. However, sulfur doping along with nitrogen, enhanced the N-state of CDs leading to synergistic effect and increased fluorescence intensity.
The TEM images (Fig. 2C) of CDs reveal the size distribution that range from 2 to 6 nm in diameter. It can be seen from TEM images that CDs are well separated and spherical in shape.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent conjugation of CDs with ssDNA and photophysical characterization
ce:section.ce:para: For hydrogel formation, the DNA sequence was designed to include C-rich region to facilitate the formation of i-motif structure in slightly acidic condition. The amine-functionalized water soluble CDs was conjugated to 5′-phosphorylated ssDNA using EDC and 1-methylimidazole as coupling agents. The terminal phosphate group of ssDNA was activated by the coupling agents and the activated ssDNA was added to CDs in HEPES buffer at alkaline pH. The DNA conjugated CDs was analyzed by UV–vis, fluorescence spectroscopy, agarose gel electrophoresis and circular dichroism. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CD-DNA conjugate show one shoulder at 270 nm due to the presence of C-rich DNA along with an absorption peak at 330 nm due to CDs (Fig. 3 A). The fluorescence spectra of CDs-DNA conjugate show slight red shift of ∼ 3 nm from 430 nm (for CDs only) to 433 nm (Fig. 3B). In agarose gel electrophoresis, the mobility of CD-DNA conjugate was lower as compared to free ssDNA or CD alone (Fig. 3C) which confirms the covalent conjugation. As a control, mixture of CD and ssDNA without conjugation was used, that shows no retardation in the CD or ssDNA mobility, both of which move independent of each other.

We further analyzed the i-motif structure of DNA and CD-DNA conjugate by circular dichroism. The circular dichroism spectra of i-motif structure formed in DNA sequences by hydrogen bonding shows signature positive peak at ∼288 nm and negative peak at ∼265 nm along with a crossover at 275 nm [49]. In our case, the circular dichroism spectra of DNA and CD-DNA conjugate exhibit a positive band at 276 nm and a negative band at 241 nm confirming the B-form of DNA for both i.e. free ssDNA and CD-DNA conjugate in alkaline condition (pH 8) (Fig. 3D). This indicates the B-conformation of DNA was maintained after conjugation with CDs in alkaline pH. The circular dichroism spectra of DNA (at pH 5, acidic condition) and CD-DNA (at pH 7, neutral condition) have a positive band at 288 nm and a negative band at 262 nm which indicates the formation of i-motif structure. Thus, the selected DNA sequence forms i-motif structure at pH 5 in free state and at pH 7 when conjugated to the CDs. These results also show that the CD-DNA conjugate can undergo intermolecular i-motif structure formation with CDs as a crosslinker between the DNA strands. Thus, conjugation of the ssDNA with CDs is not a deterrent for the pH dependent transformation of the DNA from B-conformation to i-motif structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Creation and characterization of hydrogel obtained from CD-DNA conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-DNA conjugate was processed for hydrogel formation by decreasing the pH from 8 to 7 using NaCl buffer. A neutral pH induced the protonation of cytosine and hydrogen bonding between two ssDNA which results in intermolecular i-motif formation. The structure was preserved via intercalation of CC+ base pairs between DNA strands on the surface of CDs, resulting in sol to gel transition. Optical images obtained after gel formation clearly indicate loss of fluidity and hence, successful formation of hydrogel from these DNA sequences (Fig. 4 A and B). The CD-DNA conjugate appears light yellow color in daylight while upon UV irradiation it shows blue fluorescence due to the presence of CDs.

The mechanical property of DNA hydrogel was further studied by rheological test. The hydrogel was analyzed by frequency sweep to determine the linear equilibrium modulus. The frequency dependence of shear storage (G′) and shear loss (G″) moduli are characteristic analytical properties of hydrogels. A single material can behave like solid (G′ > G″) at high frequency and like liquid (G′′ > G′) at low frequency region. Frequency sweep test was performed between 0.05 and 100 rad/s at 25 °C at a fixed strain of 1% [50]. The shear-storage modulus shows higher value than shear-loss modulus over the frequency range of 0.05–100 rad/s which confirms the gel like structure of the CD-DNA hybrid hydrogel over the entire frequency sweep (Fig. 4C). Moreover, the G′ was found to increase with increase in the frequency that is considered as characteristic signature of gel like structure. This result validated the successful formation of hydrogel with CDs as a linker. Thus, DNA hydrogel can be prepared from short DNA sequences using CDs, where the later can act as a connecting link between the building blocks of ssDNA.

Further, the interaction between CD-DNA and Dox in solution state was analyzed by time resolved fluorescence decay that was fitted to triexponential function. The average lifetime of CD-DNA conjugate and Dox (100 μg) containing CD-DNA were found to be 7.43 ns and 7.42 ns respectively, a negligible difference in lifetime of CDs on Dox encapsulation confirms the static quenching of CD-DNA with Dox (Fig. 6D and E) [53]. The Dox get trapped in close cavity of the gel through electrostatic interaction between the anthracene group of Dox and carboxyl group of CDs. The emission of Dox in the range of 500–750 nm may be convenient for in vivo imaging as it overcomes the side effects of imaging in the blue region of CDs which emit at 430 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro release profile of drug from hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the release profile of drug encapsulated in hydrogel, the drug was incubated in PBS buffer of pH 6 (cancer cell pH) and pH 7.4 (normal physiological pH) at 37 °C. The PBS solution was collected and replaced with fresh buffer for drug release at 24 h intervals. Digital images of gel were taken during drug release study which shows changes in the size of gel, degradation with time and release of drug from gel (Fig. 7 A). The release of drug from gel was calculated from the absorbance of total drug encapsulated in gel and absorbance of PBS buffer containing Dox, collected at different time intervals. The release of drug from gel is dependent on extent of cross-linking and interaction of drug with DNA [54,55]. The interaction of Dox with DNA and CDs the crosslinker in gel resist the burst release of drug by slow degradation of gel. The sustained release was observed for the gel incubated at pH 6 in 11 days that amounts to a cumulative ∼91% of the total encapsulated Dox. However, gel incubated at pH 7.4 shows total release of ∼94% in 28 days (Fig. 7B). Thus, the drug release profile confirms the faster release of Dox in acidic condition as compared to normal physiological pH. This pH dependent release of drug from gel may be attributed to decrease in electrostatic interaction between CDs and Dox on diffusion of buffer in gel [56]. Further, the disruption of the intermolecular i-motif structure in CD-DNA hydrogel in acidic pH releases the intercalated Dox from the DNA, contributing to the overall release of the drug. This indicates that the pH responsive CD-DNA hybrid hydrogel can be specifically targeted to cancer or other tissues that thrive in acidic environment to deliver a payload of drug in a sustained manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay of hydrogel with HeLa cell
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the effectiveness of the drug upon its release from hydrogel, cell cytotoxicity assay was performed using HeLa cell lines. HeLa cell lines were treated with Dox solution (20 μg/mL), hydrogel without Dox or hydrogel loaded with Dox (20 μg/mL). The viability of HeLa cells was analyzed over the time period of 72 h using MTT assay. The survival of cells treated with free Dox and Dox encapsulated in hydrogel was decreased by 79% and 65% respectively in 72 h (Fig. 8 ). However, hydrogel without Dox did not show any decrease in cell viability and hence, is nontoxic to cells. These results indicate the sustained release of drug from hydrogel for prolong drug delivery. The higher survival in case of the hydrogel-encapsulated drug also signifies that initial burst release of the drug is restricted and the drug is released slowly.

In summary, CDs mediated DNA hydrogel was used as drug delivery vehicle for sustained release of drug. The amine functionalized surface of CDs was conjugated to C-rich ssDNA and processed for network formation through the formation of DNA i-motif structure. The intense fluorescence of the CDs was retained in the hydrogel that may be potentially tracked through imaging to correlate the efficacy and level of drug loading. The hybrid CD-DNA hydrogel was found to possess viscoelastic properties in a broad frequency range of 0.05–100 rad/s. The positively charged Dox was encapsulated in the hydrogel by electrostatic interaction with the CDs as well as through intercalation in DNA. The loading of Dox in the hydrogel could be visually detected whereby the hydrogel assumes an orange color in white light and shows pink fluorescence in UV light. This may pave the way for potential in-vivo applications including tracking of the release and distribution of the drug. While persistent release of the drug was observed in acidic condition resulting from hydrogel disruption through a span of 10–11 days, the hydrogel was found to sustain for nearly a month in PBS buffer at pH 7.4 releasing the drug even more slowly. The conjugation of CD with DNA presents an economic and environment friendly way to create pH responsive hybrid hydrogel for drug delivery applications. However, we believe further research would unearth other interesting applications of the hydrogel including but not limited to cancer theranostics.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant to P. D., and M. K. Singh from BRNS, Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), Govt. of India, Project No.2012/34/59/BRNS. S. S. is thankful to BRNS India, for fellowship. The authors are thankful to IIT Patna, BARC (Mumbai), SAIF-Punjab University and NIPER Hajipur for infrastructure and experimental facilities.
